era_context: The reign of Commodus in the Roman Empire (180–192 CE), in its final phase. The Han Dynasty in China was in severe decline, with the Yellow Turban Rebellion ongoing and warlords rising. The Parthian Empire under Vologases V was stable but facing long-term decline. In India, the Satavahana Empire remained a major power. This was a period of instability and transition across multiple civilizations.

events.title: Commodus's reign continues; increasing unpopularity and erratic behavior
events.description: The year 191 CE saw Commodus continuing his reign as Roman Emperor, now in its eleventh year (he had ruled since 180 CE). By this time, Commodus was increasingly unpopular with the Senate, the military, and the people. His reign was marked by arbitrary executions, personal extravagances, and erratic behavior. He had alienated the Praetorian Guard and the Senate through his capricious rule. The empire was stable militarily and economically, but political tensions were mounting. Commodus's unpopularity would eventually lead to his assassination in late 192 CE.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire, Book 1

events.certainty_note: Commodus's reign and his unpopularity are well-attested in multiple sources. The dating to 191 CE is secure.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Yellow Turban Rebellion continues; warlords consolidate power
events.description: The Yellow Turban Rebellion, which began in 184 CE, continued to disrupt Han Dynasty authority in 191 CE. However, the rebellion had fragmented into regional uprisings rather than a unified movement. Warlords such as Cao Cao, Liu Bei, and Sun Ce were consolidating power in different regions, effectively ending Han imperial authority. The year 191 CE marked a critical point in the fragmentation of the Han Dynasty, with real power shifting from the imperial court to regional military strongmen.

sources.name: Records of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguozhi), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Book of the Later Han (Hou Han Shu)

events.certainty_note: The general fragmentation of Han authority is well-documented. Precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain; sources compiled centuries later may conflate events. The year 191 CE is approximate for this phase.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Cao Cao defeats Yellow Turban forces and expands control
events.description: Cao Cao, a rising warlord in northern China, continued his military campaigns against Yellow Turban rebels and rival warlords in 191 CE. By this year, he had secured much of the Yellow River valley and was establishing himself as the dominant military figure in the north. His victories were facilitated by superior organization and the loyalty of his troops. This period marked the transition from the Yellow Turban Rebellion to the warlord period that would lead to the Three Kingdoms.

sources.name: Records of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguozhi), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Book of the Later Han (Hou Han Shu)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Cao Cao's rise is well-documented. Precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain; sources compiled centuries later may conflate events. The year 191 CE is approximate for this phase.

events.title: Parthian Empire: Vologases V's reign; stability amid long-term decline
events.region: Parthian Empire (modern Iraq, Iran, and surrounding regions)
events.description: Vologases V continued his reign over the Parthian Empire in 191 CE. His rule was marked by attempts to maintain stability against internal fragmentation and external Roman pressure. However, the Parthian Empire was in long-term decline, and Vologases's authority was limited compared to earlier Parthian rulers. The empire faced challenges from regional powers asserting independence and from Rome's growing military strength.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Vologases V's reign is debated; estimates range from 191–209 CE to 207–222 CE. Numismatic evidence is contemporary but does not provide precise annual dating.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Vologases V's reign is disputed; some scholars place him in the early 3rd century CE rather than the late 2nd century CE. The precise dating of Cao Cao's campaigns is uncertain; sources compiled centuries later may conflate or reorder events.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 191 CE is dominated by Roman sources (Cassius Dio, Herodian, Historia Augusta), all written decades or centuries after the events. These sources are generally reliable for major events but may contain chronological errors. Chinese sources (Records of the Three Kingdoms) are similarly post-contemporary and may reorder events. Parthian sources are limited to numismatic and epigraphic evidence. The bias toward Mediterranean and East Asian history is pronounced; other regions are essentially absent. For the year 191 CE specifically, there are few major documented events; the year is significant primarily as part of broader trends (Commodus's declining popularity, Han fragmentation, warlord consolidation).

graph_edges.note: Warlord consolidation led to major battles for supremacy
Cao Cao's consolidation led to Three Kingdoms period
